content.problemStatement:
An interval is a pair of positive integers [a, b] with a ≤ b. It is meant to denote the set of integers that lie between the values a and b. For example [3,5] denotes the set {3,4,5} while the interval [3, 3] denotes the set {3}.



We say that an interval [a, b] is covered by an integer i, if i belongs to the set defined by [a, b]. For example interval [3, 5] is covered by 3 and so is the interval [3, 3].


Given a set of intervals I, and a set of integers S we say that I is covered by S if for each interval [a, b] in I there is an integer i in S such that [a, b] is covered by i. For example, the set {[3, 5], [3, 3]} is covered by the set {3}. The set of intervals {[6, 9], [3, 5], [4, 8]} is covered by the set {4, 5, 8}. It is also covered by the set {4, 7}.


We would like to compute, for any set of intervals I, the size of the smallest set S that covers it. You can check that for the set of intervals {[6, 9], [3, 5], [4, 8]} the answer is 2 while for the set of intervals {[3, 5], [3, 3]} the answer is 1.


Your program should take the set of intervals as input and output the size of the smallest set that covers it as the answer.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 5000) – the number of intervals. The next N lines each contain two integers a and b (1 ≤ a ≤ b ≤ 10^8) describing an interval [a, b].
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one integer – the size of the smallest set of integers that covers all given intervals.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Sorting by right endpoint gives [3,3] then [3,5]. The first interval forces picking point 3, which also covers the second interval. Minimum size is 1.
Sorted intervals: [3,5], [4,8], [6,9]. Pick point 5 for the first interval; it also covers the second. The third interval is not covered, so pick point 9. Two points suffice.
A single interval [1,1] requires the point 1.

solution.approach:
The problem is the classic minimum hitting set for intervals on the integer line. A greedy algorithm works: sort all intervals by their right endpoint (b) in non‑decreasing order. Scan the sorted list, keeping the last chosen point (initially -1). For each interval [a, b], if a is greater than the last chosen point, the current interval is not covered; we must select a new point. The optimal choice is the right endpoint b of this interval, because it is the farthest to the right while still covering the interval, thus maximising the chance to cover subsequent intervals. Increment the answer and update the last chosen point to b. This yields the minimum possible number of points.

Correctness follows from the exchange argument: any optimal solution can be transformed to the greedy one without increasing its size by repeatedly replacing the first point that covers the first uncovered interval with the right endpoint of that interval.

Complexities: sorting takes O(N log N) time, the scan is O(N). Memory usage is O(N) to store the intervals.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case due to sorting; overall O(T * N log N).
